stance_spectrum.description: Strongly theoretical orientation. Despite being a practicing judge and physician, argues that demonstrative philosophical knowledge is the highest human achievement. His engineering/medical work is subordinate to pure philosophical contemplation.

Slightly individualist. Defends individual philosopher's right to pursue demonstrative truth against religious authority. However, his concept of shared material intellect introduces a subtle communal dimension to cognition.

Moderately rationalist. Accepts empirical observation as starting point for natural philosophy ('demonstration of existence' from sensible features), but insists ultimate certainty requires demonstrative proof from prior causes.

Moderately innovative within tradition. Innovative in interpreting Aristotle and defending philosophy against theologians, but fundamentally loyal to Aristotelian framework. Sees himself as recovering true Aristotle, not creating new philosophy.

Religiously committed rationalist. Defends philosophy as religiously commanded, not secular. Seeks to reconcile faith and reason rather than separate them. His 'secularism' is methodological, not ideological.

key_oppositions.description: Defends philosophy's legitimacy against theologians like al-Ghazali; argues philosophers are not outside Islam; theology and philosophy are different paths to same truth
Demonstration (burhān) produces certainty; dialectical arguments are useful but less certain; each appropriate for different audiences
Follows Aristotle in holding world eternal; argues against Ash'arite creation ex nihilo; claims apparent Quranic verses support eternalist interpretation
Defends real causal powers inherent in things against al-Ghazali's occasionalism; causal relations are essential, not habitual
